function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The filing retrospectively recasts historical financials under common-control accounting following the mergers. What historical information is no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about the SpaceX S-1 filing and specifically about the retrospective recasting of historical financials under common-control accounting following the mergers. They want to know what historical information is no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment.

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand the context.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about common-control accounting, retrospective recasting of historical financials, and the mergers with xAI and X Holdings? What information is no longer observable or comparable relative to purchase accounting treatment? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
# Common-Control Accounting, Retrospective Recasting, and the xAI/X Mergers

## 1. The Merger Transactions

Two sequential share-exchange transactions were executed:

| Transaction | Date | Acquirer | Target | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **X Merger** | March 28, 2025 | xAI | X Holdings Corp. and X.AI Corp. | Share exchange; X and X.AI Corp. became wholly-owned subsidiaries of xAI |
| **xAI Merger** | February 2, 2026 | SpaceX | X.AI Holdings Corp. (xAI) | Share exchange; xAI became a wholly-owned subsidiary of SpaceX |

X.AI Corp. began operations in March 2023; Twitter, Inc. was acquired by Elon Musk in October 2022. Both mergers were effected through share exchanges, and the combined transactions are referred to collectively as the "Mergers."

---

## 2. Common-Control Accounting Treatment

### Basis for Common-Control Treatment

The Mergers were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control** rather than as business combinations under ASC 805 (purchase accounting). The justification stated in the filing is:

> "Mr. Elon Musk had a controlling financial interest in the Company, xAI and X through his majority voting interest in each such entity during the years presented in these consolidated financial statements."

Because Musk held majority voting interests across all three entities throughout all periods presented, the transactions did not represent a change in ultimate control and therefore qualified for common-control pooling treatment.

### Accounting Consequences

The filing specifies the following consequences of common-control treatment:

1. **Retrospective combination at historical carrying amounts**: The consolidated financial statements reflect the combination of the net assets of SpaceX, xAI, and X at their **historical carrying amounts** for all periods presented — not at fair value.

2. **No new goodwill or intangible assets**: The filing explicitly states: *"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded."* This is a direct consequence of using historical book values rather than fair value purchase price allocation.

3. **Elimination of intercompany transactions**: *"All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation."* This means all transactions that occurred between SpaceX, xAI, and X during historical periods — such as commercial arrangements, service agreements, or funding transfers — are removed from the consolidated financials as if they never occurred.

4. **Equity continuation and adjustment**: The capital stock and shareholders' equity for all periods presented reflects:
   - A continuation of historical SpaceX capital stock and shareholders' equity, **combined with** the historical capital stock and shareholders' equity of X and xAI, **as adjusted by the respective exchange ratios** used to effect the Mergers.
   - The one exception is xAI's historical redeemable convertible preferred stock, which is discussed below.

5. **Change in reporting entity**: The filing explicitly states: *"This presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity."* This is a significant accounting designation indicating that the reporting entity has fundamentally changed — the financial statements now encompass entities that were previously separate.

6. **No separate financial statements provided**: The filing states: *"As the consolidated financial statements already reflect the reorganization of entities under common control for all periods presented, separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided."* This is a direct consequence of the retrospective combination — because the historical financials already include all entities, standalone statements would be redundant.

---

## 3. Retrospective Recasting of Historical Financials

The filing describes the retrospective recasting in multiple locations:

> "The consolidated financial statements of SpaceX have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include (i) the historical results of X.AI Holdings Corp., which was acquired by SpaceX, effective February 2, 2026 (the 'xAI Merger'), and X Holdings Corp. ('X Holdings'), which was acquired by xAI, effective March 28, 2025 (the 'X Merger'), because these transactions were between entities under common control, and (ii) a five-for-one stock split of the Company's Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock, effective May 4, 2026 (the '2026 Stock Split')."

### Scope of Retrospective Recasting

The recasting applies to:
- **All periods presented** in the consolidated financial statements
- The historical results of both xAI and X Holdings
- All share and per share information (retroactively adjusted for the 2026 Stock Split)

### Exchange Ratios Applied Retrospectively

The filing details the specific exchange ratios that were applied to retroactively convert historical share counts:

**X Merger (March 28, 2025):**
| Security Converted | Conversion Ratio | Into |
|---|---|---|
| X Holdings Corp. Common Stock (each class) | 2.776 shares | xAI Common Stock (same class), rounded down |
| X.AI Corp. Common Stock (each class) | 1.000 share | xAI Common Stock (same class) |
| X.AI Corp. Preferred Stock (each series, excluding shares held by X or its subsidiaries) | 1.000 share | xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock |

**xAI Merger (February 2, 2026):**
| Security Converted | Conversion Ratio | Into |
|---|---|---|
| xAI Common Stock (Class A, B, or Limited Voting) | 0.1433 SpaceX shares | SpaceX Common Stock |
| xAI Common Stock (eligible service provider cash election) | $75.46 per share | Cash (pre-2026 Stock Split basis) |
| xAI customer warrants (vested and unvested) | 0.1433 (cashless exercise) | SpaceX Class A Common Stock (fully vested) |
| xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock | SpaceX Class B Common Stock per preferred share (pre-2026 Stock Split basis) | SpaceX Common Stock |

The filing states:

> "As a result of the Mergers, all of X, X.AI Corp. and xAI Common Stock are being presented in the historical financial statements as if they had been converted into SpaceX Common Stock at the applicable exchange rate for all periods presented through the date of the xAI Merger."

### The One Exception: xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock

The filing identifies a critical exception to the retrospective recasting. The xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was **not** retrospectively recast on an as-converted basis for periods prior to the xAI Merger Date. The filing explains:

> "Because the xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was legally outstanding during all historical periods prior to the xAI Merger and represented a separate equity class of a legally distinct predecessor entity, the conversion of xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock into SpaceX Common Stock is recognized only as of the closing of the xAI Merger, and not retrospectively."

Accordingly:
- Historical consolidated balance sheets and statements of redeemable convertible preferred stock and shareholders' equity reflect the xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock **as outstanding xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** consistent with its legal form and rights during those periods.
- The conversion impact is presented **prospectively** in the period of the merger (Q1 2026), specifically in the consolidated statements of redeemable convertible preferred stock and shareholders' equity for the three months ended March 31, 2026.
- All shares of xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock were converted to SpaceX common stock as part of the xAI Merger and are presented as such **from the date of the xAI Merger forward**.

---

## 4. What Information Is No Longer Observable or Comparable Relative to Purchase Accounting Treatment

Under purchase accounting (ASC 805), an acquirer would record the acquired entity's assets and liabilities at **fair value**, recognize **goodwill** for the excess of consideration over fair value of net assets, recognize **identifiable intangible assets**, and include the acquiree's results only **from the acquisition date forward**. The use of common-control accounting instead of purchase accounting means the following information is **no longer observable or comparable**:

### a. Fair Value of Acquired Net Assets
Under purchase accounting, X's and xAI's tangible and intangible assets and liabilities would have been recorded at fair value at the acquisition date. Under common-control accounting, they are recorded at **historical carrying amounts**. The fair values of X's platform, user base, brand, technology infrastructure, and xAI's AI models, computational infrastructure, and intellectual property are **not reflected** in the consolidated financial statements and cannot be derived from them.

### b. Goodwill
The filing explicitly states that **no new goodwill was recorded**. Under purchase accounting, the excess of the purchase consideration over the fair value of identifiable net assets would have been recorded as goodwill. Because the mergers were structured as share exchanges among commonly controlled entities, the implied purchase price premium — and any resulting goodwill — is **not observable**. An investor cannot determine what premium, if any, was effectively paid for X or xAI.

### c. Identifiable Intangible Assets
Under purchase accounting, identifiable intangibles such as X's trade name, user relationships, advertiser relationships, developed technology, and xAI's AI models and algorithms would have been separately recognized at fair value and amortized over their useful lives. Under common-control accounting, **no new intangible assets were recorded**. These assets remain embedded within the historical carrying amounts of the acquired entities and are **not separately identifiable**.

### d. Standalone Financial Performance of xAI and X
Because the financial statements are retrospectively combined for all periods, and because the filing explicitly states that **separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided**, an investor cannot:
- Determine the standalone revenue, expenses, or net income/loss of xAI or X for any historical period
- Assess the individual profitability or growth trajectory of the AI business versus the social media platform
- Compare pre-merger standalone performance to post-merger combined performance
- Evaluate whether the mergers created or destroyed value on a standalone basis

### e. Intercompany Transaction History
The filing states that **all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation**. This means that any commercial arrangements, service agreements, data-sharing arrangements, compute provisioning, advertising arrangements, or funding transfers between SpaceX, xAI, and X during historical periods are **not visible** in the consolidated financials. An investor cannot assess the nature, volume, or economics of these inter-entity relationships prior to formal combination.

Note: Transactions with **external** related parties (such as Tesla and The Boring Company) remain visible because those entities are not part of the consolidated group. For example, the filing discloses that xAI obtained goods and services from Tesla of $191 million in 2024 and $506 million in 2025, and X Holdings had advertising revenue from Tesla of $0.5 million in 2024 and $4 million in 2025.

### f. Fair Value Step-Up and Its Effect on Future Depreciation/Amortization
Under purchase accounting, the fair value step-up in acquired assets would have increased the depreciation and amortization expense in future periods. Under common-control accounting, depreciation and amortization continue to be based on historical carrying amounts. The financial statements therefore do **not reflect** the higher depreciation/amortization that would have resulted from purchase accounting, making future expense trends **not comparable** to what they would have been under purchase accounting.

### g. Purchase Price Allocation
Under purchase accounting, a purchase price allocation would have been disclosed showing how the total consideration was allocated among assets acquired, liabilities assumed, goodwill, and intangible assets. Under common-control accounting, **no purchase price allocation is provided** because the transaction is accounted for at historical carrying amounts. The implied valuation of the share exchanges (based on the exchange ratios and the value of SpaceX shares) is **not reconciled** to the net assets received.

### h. Pro Forma Combined Results vs. Actual Combined Results
Under purchase accounting, the filing would have included pro forma financial information showing what the combined entity's results would have been (with fair value adjustments) as if the merger had occurred at the beginning of the earliest period presented. Under common-control accounting, the **actual** historical combined results (at historical carrying amounts, without fair value adjustments) are presented instead. While this provides actual combined data, it is **not comparable** to what pro forma purchase-accounting results would have shown, because it lacks the fair value adjustments, goodwill amortization, and intangible asset amortization that would have been included.

### i. Acquisition-Date Balance Sheet
Under purchase accounting, an acquisition-date balance sheet would show the fair values of assets acquired and liabilities assumed. Under common-control accounting, the balance sheet simply **continues** the historical carrying amounts of all entities, with equity adjusted for exchange ratios. There is no "step-up" or revaluation at the acquisition date, so the investor **cannot observe** the fair value balance sheet of the acquired entities at the merger date.

### j. Earn-Out or Contingent Consideration
The filing does not discuss contingent consideration or earn-outs, but under purchase accounting, any contingent consideration would have been recognized at fair value. Under common-control accounting, such arrangements (if any existed) would be accounted for differently and may **not be observable** in the same way.

---

## 5. Summary of Key Distinctions

| Aspect | Purchase Accounting (ASC 805) | Common-Control Accounting (As Applied) |
|---|---|---|
| Asset/liability measurement | Fair value at acquisition date | Historical carrying amounts |
| Goodwill | Recognized for excess of consideration over fair value of net assets | **Not recorded** |
| Identifiable intangibles | Recognized at fair value, amortized over useful lives | **Not recorded** |
| Reporting periods | Acquiree included from acquisition date forward | **Retrospectively combined for all periods presented** |
| Intercompany transactions | Eliminated only from acquisition date forward | **Eliminated for all historical periods** |
| Separate financial statements of acquiree | Typically provided or pro forma disclosed | **Not provided** |
| Purchase price allocation | Disclosed | **Not applicable/not disclosed** |
| Pro forma financial information | Required | **Not provided** (actual combined results presented instead) |
| Change in reporting entity | Not typically designated | **Explicitly designated as a change in reporting entity** |
| Depreciation/amortization going forward | Reflects fair value step-up | **Based on historical carrying amounts** |
| xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock | Would be fair-valued/converted at acquisition | **Not retrospectively recast; presented in legal form until xAI Merger Date, then converted prospectively** |

The net effect is that the consolidated financial statements present a combined entity whose historical financials reflect the actual historical carrying amounts and operating results of SpaceX, xAI, and X as if they had always been combined — but without any fair value revaluation, goodwill recognition, intangible asset identification, or purchase price allocation that would have provided insight into the economic value assigned to the acquired businesses through the share exchange transactions.

steps.message: I have a comprehensive understanding of the filing's treatment. Let me verify a few specific details about the disclosures to ensure accuracy.
steps.reasoning_content: The retrieval gave me a comprehensive answer. Let me also verify some specific details by retrieving more information about the specific accounting treatment and what's no longer observable. Let me also check the specific language in the filing about the change in reporting entity and what's not provided.

arguments.prompt: What specific language does the filing use regarding: (1) the change in reporting entity designation, (2) the statement that separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided, (3) the elimination of intercompany transactions, and (4) the absence of goodwill and intangible assets? Quote the exact language from the filing. {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
# Exact Language from the Filing

## (1) Change in Reporting Entity Designation

> **"This presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity."**

This sentence appears twice in the filing — once referencing Note 13 and once referencing Note 12 — depending on the version of the preamble section.

## (2) Separate Financial Statements of xAI and X Not Provided

> **"As the consolidated financial statements already reflect the reorganization of entities under common control for all periods presented, separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided."**

## (3) Elimination of Intercompany Transactions

The filing addresses this in two distinct locations:

- In the merger accounting discussion: **"all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation"**

- In Note 2 (Summary of Significant Accounting Policies): **"All intercompany balances and transactions have been eliminated in consolidation."**

## (4) Absence of Goodwill and Intangible Assets

> **"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded"**

This phrase appears in conjunction with the elimination of intercompany transactions, forming a single sentence: *"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded and all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation."*

steps.message: I have sufficient information to provide a comprehensive answer.
steps.reasoning_content:
I have all the information I need to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me now structure my final response.

The question asks: "The filing retrospectively recasts historical financials under common-control accounting following the mergers. What historical information is no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment?"

I need to focus on what's no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment. The key items are:

1. Fair value of acquired net assets (X and xAI's assets/liabilities at fair value)
2. Goodwill (no new goodwill recorded)
3. Identifiable intangible assets (no new intangibles recorded)
4. Standalone financial performance of xAI and X (separate financial statements not provided)
5. Intercompany transaction history (eliminated for all historical periods)
6. Fair value step-up and its effect on future depreciation/amortization
7. Purchase price allocation (not applicable/not disclosed)
8. Pro forma combined results (actual combined results presented instead)
9. Acquisition-date balance sheet (no step-up)
10. The xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock exception (not retrospectively recast)

Let me structure this answer clearly.

arguments.final_result:
## What Historical Information Is No Longer Observable Under Common-Control Accounting vs. Purchase Accounting

The SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) retrospectively recasts the consolidated financial statements to include xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all periods presented, because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X throughout the periods presented. The filing explicitly states: *"This presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity."*

Because the Mergers were accounted for as reorganizations of entities under common control rather than as business combinations under ASC 805 (purchase accounting), the following historical information is **no longer observable or comparable** relative to what purchase accounting would have produced:

### 1. Fair Value of Acquired Net Assets
Under purchase accounting, X's and xAI's assets and liabilities would have been recorded at **fair value** at the acquisition date. Under common-control accounting, they are recorded at **historical carrying amounts**. The fair values of X's platform, user base, brand, technology infrastructure, and xAI's AI models, computational infrastructure, and intellectual property are not reflected and cannot be derived from the consolidated financials.

### 2. Goodwill
The filing explicitly states: *"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded."* Under purchase accounting, the excess of consideration over the fair value of identifiable net assets would have been recorded as goodwill. The implied purchase price premium paid for X or xAI — and any resulting goodwill — is therefore **not observable**.

### 3. Identifiable Intangible Assets
Under purchase accounting, identifiable intangibles (e.g., X's trade name, user/advertiser relationships, developed technology; xAI's AI models and algorithms) would have been separately recognized at fair value and amortized. Under common-control accounting, **no new intangible assets were recorded**. These assets remain embedded within historical carrying amounts and are **not separately identifiable**.

### 4. Standalone Financial Performance of xAI and X
The filing states: *"As the consolidated financial statements already reflect the reorganization of entities under common control for all periods presented, separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided."* As a result, an investor cannot observe:
- Standalone revenue, expenses, or net income/loss of xAI or X for any historical period
- The individual profitability or growth trajectory of the AI business versus the social media platform
- Pre-merger standalone performance versus post-merger combined performance
- Whether the mergers created or destroyed value on a standalone basis

### 5. Intercompany Transaction History
The filing states that *"all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation"* (and Note 2 confirms: *"All intercompany balances and transactions have been eliminated in consolidation"*). Under purchase accounting, intercompany transactions would only be eliminated from the acquisition date forward. Under common-control accounting, they are eliminated for **all historical periods**, so any commercial arrangements, service agreements, data-sharing, compute provisioning, advertising arrangements, or funding transfers between SpaceX, xAI, and X during historical periods are **not visible** in the consolidated financials.

(Note: Transactions with external related parties such as Tesla and The Boring Company remain visible — e.g., xAI obtained goods/services from Tesla of $191M in 2024 and $506M in 2025; X had advertising revenue from Tesla of $0.5M in 2024 and $4M in 2025.)

### 6. Fair Value Step-Up and Its Effect on Future Depreciation/Amortization
Under purchase accounting, the fair value step-up in acquired assets would have increased future depreciation and amortization expense. Under common-control accounting, depreciation and amortization continue to be based on historical carrying amounts, so future expense trends are **not comparable** to what they would have been under purchase accounting.

### 7. Purchase Price Allocation
Under purchase accounting, a purchase price allocation would have been disclosed showing how total consideration was allocated among assets acquired, liabilities assumed, goodwill, and intangibles. Under common-control accounting, **no purchase price allocation is provided**. The implied valuation of the share exchanges (based on the disclosed exchange ratios and SpaceX share values) is **not reconciled** to the net assets received.

### 8. Pro Forma Combined Results (with Fair Value Adjustments)
Under purchase accounting, the filing would have included pro forma financial information showing combined results as if the merger had occurred at the beginning of the earliest period presented, including fair value adjustments. Under common-control accounting, the **actual** historical combined results (at historical carrying amounts, without fair value adjustments) are presented instead. While this provides actual combined data, it is **not comparable** to what pro forma purchase-accounting results would have shown.

### 9. Acquisition-Date Balance Sheet (Fair Value)
Under purchase accounting, an acquisition-date balance sheet would show the fair values of assets acquired and liabilities assumed. Under common-control accounting, the balance sheet simply **continues** the historical carrying amounts of all entities, with equity adjusted for exchange ratios. There is no "step-up" or revaluation at the acquisition date, so the investor **cannot observe** the fair value balance sheet of the acquired entities at the merger date.

### 10. The xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock Exception
The filing identifies one notable exception to the retrospective recasting. The xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was **not** retrospectively recast on an as-converted basis for periods prior to the xAI Merger Date. The filing explains: *"Because the xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was legally outstanding during all historical periods prior to the xAI Merger and represented a separate equity class of a legally distinct predecessor entity, the conversion of xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock into SpaceX Common Stock is recognized only as of the closing of the xAI Merger, and not retrospectively."* This means the conversion impact is presented **prospectively** in Q1 2026, and the historical equity structure does not reflect the as-converted SpaceX share count for that security.

---

### Summary Table

| Item | Purchase Accounting (ASC 805) | Common-Control Accounting (As Applied) |
|---|---|---|
| Asset/liability measurement | Fair value at acquisition date | Historical carrying amounts |
| Goodwill | Recognized | **Not recorded** |
| Identifiable intangibles | Recognized at fair value, amortized | **Not recorded** |
| Reporting periods for acquiree | From acquisition date forward | **Retrospectively combined for all periods** |
| Intercompany transactions | Eliminated from acquisition date forward | **Eliminated for all historical periods** |
| Separate financial statements of acquiree | Typically provided or pro forma disclosed | **Not provided** |
| Purchase price allocation | Disclosed | **Not applicable/not disclosed** |
| Pro forma financial information | Required | **Not provided** (actual combined results instead) |
| Acquisition-date fair value balance sheet | Disclosed | **Not observable** |
| Future depreciation/amortization | Reflects fair value step-up | **Based on historical carrying amounts** |
| xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock | Would be fair-valued/converted at acquisition | **Not retrospectively recast; converted prospectively at xAI Merger Date** |

The net effect is that the consolidated financial statements present a combined entity whose historical financials reflect the actual historical carrying amounts and operating results of SpaceX, xAI, and X as if they had always been combined — but without any fair value revaluation, goodwill recognition, intangible asset identification, purchase price allocation, or standalone acquiree financials that would have provided insight into the economic value assigned to the acquired businesses through the share exchange transactions.

---

      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements, Merger Accounting discussion, and Note 2 (Summary of Significant Accounting Policies)"

results.content:
## What Historical Information Is No Longer Observable Under Common-Control Accounting vs. Purchase Accounting

The SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) retrospectively recasts the consolidated financial statements to include xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all periods presented, because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X throughout the periods presented. The filing explicitly states: *"This presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity."*

Because the Mergers were accounted for as reorganizations of entities under common control rather than as business combinations under ASC 805 (purchase accounting), the following historical information is **no longer observable or comparable** relative to what purchase accounting would have produced:

### 1. Fair Value of Acquired Net Assets
Under purchase accounting, X's and xAI's assets and liabilities would have been recorded at **fair value** at the acquisition date. Under common-control accounting, they are recorded at **historical carrying amounts**. The fair values of X's platform, user base, brand, technology infrastructure, and xAI's AI models, computational infrastructure, and intellectual property are not reflected and cannot be derived from the consolidated financials.

### 2. Goodwill
The filing explicitly states: *"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded."* Under purchase accounting, the excess of consideration over the fair value of identifiable net assets would have been recorded as goodwill. The implied purchase price premium paid for X or xAI — and any resulting goodwill — is therefore **not observable**.

### 3. Identifiable Intangible Assets
Under purchase accounting, identifiable intangibles (e.g., X's trade name, user/advertiser relationships, developed technology; xAI's AI models and algorithms) would have been separately recognized at fair value and amortized. Under common-control accounting, **no new intangible assets were recorded**. These assets remain embedded within historical carrying amounts and are **not separately identifiable**.

### 4. Standalone Financial Performance of xAI and X
The filing states: *"As the consolidated financial statements already reflect the reorganization of entities under common control for all periods presented, separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided."* As a result, an investor cannot observe:
- Standalone revenue, expenses, or net income/loss of xAI or X for any historical period
- The individual profitability or growth trajectory of the AI business versus the social media platform
- Pre-merger standalone performance versus post-merger combined performance
- Whether the mergers created or destroyed value on a standalone basis

### 5. Intercompany Transaction History
The filing states that *"all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation"* (and Note 2 confirms: *"All intercompany balances and transactions have been eliminated in consolidation"*). Under purchase accounting, intercompany transactions would only be eliminated from the acquisition date forward. Under common-control accounting, they are eliminated for **all historical periods**, so any commercial arrangements, service agreements, data-sharing, compute provisioning, advertising arrangements, or funding transfers between SpaceX, xAI, and X during historical periods are **not visible** in the consolidated financials.

(Note: Transactions with external related parties such as Tesla and The Boring Company remain visible — e.g., xAI obtained goods/services from Tesla of $191M in 2024 and $506M in 2025; X had advertising revenue from Tesla of $0.5M in 2024 and $4M in 2025.)

### 6. Fair Value Step-Up and Its Effect on Future Depreciation/Amortization
Under purchase accounting, the fair value step-up in acquired assets would have increased future depreciation and amortization expense. Under common-control accounting, depreciation and amortization continue to be based on historical carrying amounts, so future expense trends are **not comparable** to what they would have been under purchase accounting.

### 7. Purchase Price Allocation
Under purchase accounting, a purchase price allocation would have been disclosed showing how total consideration was allocated among assets acquired, liabilities assumed, goodwill, and intangibles. Under common-control accounting, **no purchase price allocation is provided**. The implied valuation of the share exchanges (based on the disclosed exchange ratios and SpaceX share values) is **not reconciled** to the net assets received.

### 8. Pro Forma Combined Results (with Fair Value Adjustments)
Under purchase accounting, the filing would have included pro forma financial information showing combined results as if the merger had occurred at the beginning of the earliest period presented, including fair value adjustments. Under common-control accounting, the **actual** historical combined results (at historical carrying amounts, without fair value adjustments) are presented instead. While this provides actual combined data, it is **not comparable** to what pro forma purchase-accounting results would have shown.

### 9. Acquisition-Date Balance Sheet (Fair Value)
Under purchase accounting, an acquisition-date balance sheet would show the fair values of assets acquired and liabilities assumed. Under common-control accounting, the balance sheet simply **continues** the historical carrying amounts of all entities, with equity adjusted for exchange ratios. There is no "step-up" or revaluation at the acquisition date, so the investor **cannot observe** the fair value balance sheet of the acquired entities at the merger date.

### 10. The xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock Exception
The filing identifies one notable exception to the retrospective recasting. The xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was **not** retrospectively recast on an as-converted basis for periods prior to the xAI Merger Date. The filing explains: *"Because the xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was legally outstanding during all historical periods prior to the xAI Merger and represented a separate equity class of a legally distinct predecessor entity, the conversion of xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock into SpaceX Common Stock is recognized only as of the closing of the xAI Merger, and not retrospectively."* This means the conversion impact is presented **prospectively** in Q1 2026, and the historical equity structure does not reflect the as-converted SpaceX share count for that security.

---

### Summary Table

| Item | Purchase Accounting (ASC 805) | Common-Control Accounting (As Applied) |
|---|---|---|
| Asset/liability measurement | Fair value at acquisition date | Historical carrying amounts |
| Goodwill | Recognized | **Not recorded** |
| Identifiable intangibles | Recognized at fair value, amortized | **Not recorded** |
| Reporting periods for acquiree | From acquisition date forward | **Retrospectively combined for all periods** |
| Intercompany transactions | Eliminated from acquisition date forward | **Eliminated for all historical periods** |
| Separate financial statements of acquiree | Typically provided or pro forma disclosed | **Not provided** |
| Purchase price allocation | Disclosed | **Not applicable/not disclosed** |
| Pro forma financial information | Required | **Not provided** (actual combined results instead) |
| Acquisition-date fair value balance sheet | Disclosed | **Not observable** |
| Future depreciation/amortization | Reflects fair value step-up | **Based on historical carrying amounts** |
| xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock | Would be fair-valued/converted at acquisition | **Not retrospectively recast; converted prospectively at xAI Merger Date** |

The net effect is that the consolidated financial statements present a combined entity whose historical financials reflect the actual historical carrying amounts and operating results of SpaceX, xAI, and X as if they had always been combined — but without any fair value revaluation, goodwill recognition, intangible asset identification, purchase price allocation, or standalone acquiree financials that would have provided insight into the economic value assigned to the acquired businesses through the share exchange transactions.
